One drought-stricken summer, he approached the Great Eagle, offering a magical gourd that supposedly poured endless water. "But first," Anoki grinned, "I need your sharpest feathers to weave a basket for the spirits."

Trusting him, the Eagle plucked his own flight feathers, leaving himself grounded. Anoki took the feathers, laughed, and revealed the gourd held only dust. He vanished, leaving the Eagle flightless and thirsty.

But the Sky Father saw this cruelty. He did not simply strike Anoki down. Instead, he decreed that for Anoki to be punished, the Eagle must carry the weight of the trickster’s deceit. The Sky Father restored the Eagle’s flight but bound Anoki to the Eagle’s shadow.

Now, whenever the Eagle soared, he felt the heavy, dragging weight of the coyote. It was exhausting, slowing his hunt and straining his wings. Yet, the Eagle endured the burden, for as long as Anoki was his shadow, the trickster could never take physical form to deceive another. The cycle ended not with revenge, but with the Eagle’s quiet sacrifice, bearing the heavy cost of keeping a liar from the world.
